text: This reminds me of a spreadsheet on my old NeXT computer. I found this video of a <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TbsfvdZXE7s|Lotus Improv Demo>. The feature I'm referring to is shown starting at 4:19.

text:
this is thought provoking; I’m struggling to understand the oppositional relationship set up, though -- why LEGO vs. FP/Types (also noting that FP and Types seem to be kinda conflated into each other)

the reason I found this so thought provoking is that when I’ve taught types to folks in the past, my central analogy to do so was LEGO. From my perch, LEGO’s are highly composable _because_ they’re also strictly typed.

Each LEGO defines a strict, immutable type which we can type-check through its physical constraints, whereas if LEGO has no types, it’d be play-doh, still powerful and expressive, but composable in a very different way.

Unlike many type systems, though, LEGO are simple and don’t require you to create adapters or complicated translating chains -- which isn’t true for all type systems, but, I think maybe LEGO can suggest a simpler, deeply expressive way of creating a type system…maybe rather than merely a point of friction types could enable the LEGO dream (which I defo share) that you express in your piece?

they’re also strictly typed.

Each LEGO defines a strict, immutable type which we can type-check through its physical constraints, whereas if LEGO has no types, it’d be play-doh, still powerful and expressive, but composable in a very different way.

Unlike many type systems, though, LEGO are simple and don’t require you to create adapters or complicated translating chains -- which isn’t true for all type systems, but, I think maybe LEGO can suggest a simpler, deeply expressive way of creating a type system…maybe rather than merely a point of friction types could enable the LEGO dream (which I defo share) that you express in your piece?

text:
For a more grounded example of what I mean, I love JS -- by default, JS has no types, and is very very Play Doh in vibe.

Wanting a more LEGO experience, though, over the last few years <https://git.sr.ht/~eli_oat/b|I’ve built up this functional programming library for JS> that, I think, helps to increase JS’ composability. It does this by enforcing some strict function signatures, and introducing 1 type.

<https://git.sr.ht/~eli_oat/b/tree/main/item/learn-b-in-y.md|This document does a better job highlighting how b.js is different from normal JS.>

elements.text:
For a more grounded example of what I mean, I love JS -- by default, JS has no types, and is very very Play Doh in vibe.

Wanting a more LEGO experience, though, over the last few years
I’ve built up this functional programming library for JS
that, I think, helps to increase JS’ composability. It does this by enforcing some strict function signatures, and introducing 1 type.
This document does a better job highlighting how b.js is different from normal JS.

text: LEGO vs. play-doh is not a good metaphor for static vs. dynamic types, in my opinion. LEGO constraints are more like placing things on a grid rather than freely. It's discretization rather than categorization. You don't define your own types in LEGO.

elements.text: LEGO vs. play-doh is not a good metaphor for static vs. dynamic types, in my opinion. LEGO constraints are more like placing things on a grid rather than freely. It's discretization rather than categorization. You don't define your own types in LEGO.

text: As for composability, typed FP gives you composability in the small but is a barrier to composability in the large. If you need to assemble parts that express the same concept in differently named or structured types, you need to start a serious fight against the type checker.

elements.text: As for composability, typed FP gives you composability in the small but is a barrier to composability in the large. If you need to assemble parts that express the same concept in differently named or structured types, you need to start a serious fight against the type checker.
